sentences.text: Pre train ride, not really, no.
There were a lot of children--
Yes.
A lot.
It was a trainful.
I don't know how many, but I would say in the hundreds.
Certainly in the hundreds.
I understand that a total of 10,000 children got out that way.
And I don't know how many transports were.
Otherwise, one could divide one by the other, and one could get the average number.
But certainly in the hundreds, there were.
There were several hundred children there.
This was, I believe, the first transport.
I did know of it at that time as a Kindertransport.
I did not know the origin of it, how this was.
In a way, this was almost like a-- like a kind of miracle, for me.
I felt it as a kind of miracle.
Because literally one day, you have absolutely no idea.
You're a teenager, early teenager.
You have literally no idea whether you're going to be alive or not a few years from now, where you're going to be.
It just-- the future is something you prefer not to think about, because the present is so awful.
And you can't get out.
That much we knew.
It was very, very difficult to get out of Germany, because other countries wouldn't take us.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew only later that, following Kristallnacht-- following October 10 and November 10-- the British parliament passed a law-- not a law, they promulgated their decision, or rather, the cabinet did, at the behest of the parliament, after debate, they didn't need a law-- a cabinet decision to admit 10,000 Jewish children from Germany and Austria.
Austria, by that time, had become part of so-called Greater Germany, as of March '38.
And they said, we will organize the, the-- with the Jewish authorities, here in England, we will organize this side.
But, of course, you guys over there have to organize your side.

At that time-- and I learnt this only weeks ago, on the occasion of seeing a film that was, it's called Kindertransport, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was shown here in Washington, just, just a few weeks ago, in early '97.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were some people from the-- some of the young professionals that had been thrown out of their professional-- out of the professions.
And some of the people from the Jewish community authorities got together, made up some committees that had to work very fast.
And don't forget, this was all-- they were definitely under the gun, as far as time was concerned.
Because they knew that it couldn't be much longer, with the war coming.
And they-- and, oh, they had to get the agreement of the Germans, number one.
They did get it, for-- somehow.
I don't know how much money that cost, but-- how many bribes it cost.
But they got it.
And then the details had to be negotiated.

Who should go, how to handle the applications, how to let people know that this exists-- all the myriad administrative details. 'Administrative detail' was a-- is a term that, lucky for me, at that time I did not even know what it meant.
I've learnt in the meantime what it means.
[LAUGHS] But at that time, all these things were just totally beyond me.
And that's why I use the term 'miracle.' In a way, it was a kind of miracle.
It was the only country-- and this needs to be said-- the only country at that time that made this-- England was the only country that made this gesture that saved, at the very least, 10,000 lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for that I have always-- in England, out of England-- I have always had a very thankful feeling and respectful feeling for, for the English.
The lower limit, I can't say.
From observation and from memory, I would say seven or eight.
Maybe it was six.
The upper limit was 16.
That I recall.
But most of us were sort of in between-- between, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 9 to 13, 10 to 13, 14.
Um, well, this is a funny thing.
When the Kindertransport [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Association was formed in England-- a few years ago, on the 50th anniversary-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time I was asked the same question.
And I said, I just remember boys!
I don't remember girls.
[LAUGHS] And I have nothing against girls, believe me.
This was not-- I had not the, the maybe typical, at a certain age, aversion to-- oh, girls!
No, that was never the case with me.
So I would have known.
And yet, everybody assures me that they were all mixed.
So I, I can't answer that.
I don't know.
All I can say is, as far as I know we were all boys.
That particular transport was all boys.
There may have been others that were mixed.
And it turns out that this was the first one that-- the first transport that left.
To my knowledge, they were all Jews.
[DRINKS] We were-- had been limited, by the government, to one suitcase each.
Well, that could be carried-- nothing of those big ones on wheels-- that could be hand-carried. 10 marks.
And absolute [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rule against any valuables, any precious stones, any jewelry, anything-- any valuables, in brief.
And some of the children, with well-meaning but misguided parents, had, had things, valuables, sewn into their clothing.
It was a stupid and reckless thing.
I can understand it, yes.
What is my little one going to do without anything?
It's better for the little one to get out alive and poor than with the jewelry and perish in the attempt.
In our train, there was one commotion that reportedly at the border, when the SS came and searched everything.
Took the suitcases, smashed some of them on the-- just thew them on the platform-- emptied them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Something that apparently for future transports, there were presentations made to the-- representation made to the government.
And they apparently stopped the worst of that, for the future transport.
But on my transport, there was a lot of that going on.
They searched whom they wanted to, whoever looked suspicious to them.
Who they didn't like the looks of, they would be very brutal about that.
In my-- in my section, we were very lucky.
Nothing like that happened there.
They got the suitcases down but just barely opened them.
And then they just-- ah-- went on.
Then-- but we-- this commotion reportedly was a boy who had been discovered to have some jewelry in his clothing.
And reportedly he was taken off the train.
But I don't know what happened to him.
I don't even whether the story is true, or whether it's the kind of thing that runs-- rumor runs the-- along the train.
By the time that it gets to you, it's, you know, it's a different story.
[DRINKS]
Not really, not that I can recall.
There were one or two.
But they were all in the railway uniforms.
The, the railway officials and railway employees had uniforms.
Everybody had uniforms in Germany.
Yes, yes, it was.
No, no, this was definitely-- so this was a-- it was chartered, I suppose.
It had to be paid for.
And-- but, but it was done.
It was-- it's a miracle.
Amazing, an amazing job that these people did.
Oh, I would say, best of my recollection, maybe eight hours, maybe a little more.
There were stops.
We weren't the most urgent cargo that the, that the German trains were-- according to their lights-- that they were ferrying that day.
I must quickly go to the-- to the border crossing and away from the SS, if I may.
And to give another vote of thanks to the warm-- when we crossed out of, out of Germany, that was one thing.
They had a little no-man's land, and then immediately you're in Holland.
And the-- my vote of thanks goes to the wonderfully warm-hearted ladies of the, of the-- I don't know what they were.
I don't know the organization, the Jewish organization, in Holland-- that were waiting for us, that streamed through the corridors, hugging everybody, handing out cups of cocoa-- great touch, cocoa!
[LAUGHS] Hot chocolate.
And, I mean, it was-- and that's when many children broke down and really cried, that's when the let themselves.
Because you don't want to let yourself, before the others, and before you really know that you're out, because you don't want to break down for nothing.
Most of us, I am sure, like I, believed, hey, this miracle is not going to last.
We are not going to get past that border.
That border town, border post, called [GERMAN] was, in, in folklore, in Jewish community folklore, as 'the' magic place.
Once you get past that, you're out of this.
So we were actually going through [GERMAN] And then we were actually going-- we were moving out of [GERMAN] And the SS were left behind.
That was the most-- one of the most amazing moments of my positive moments of my life.
Did it cause you to break down, as well?
Or you don't remember?
I don't remember.
God, we were so well-disciplined.
[LAUGHS] I don't remember, I couldn't tell you.
I wouldn't be surprised.
We went by train to the Dutch port, Hoek van Holland.
And that same day, that same evening, we caught the night train-- night boat, I'm sorry-- to, to Harwich, the port of Harwich, in Essex, in southeastern England.
And we landed there in the morning.
This was December, bitterly cold, bitterly cold.
Wind.
Not that much snow, yet.
There was more, later.
And then we were put on buses, and we were taken to a camp.

Not a concentration camp, but a holiday camp, which had been given to the Jewish committee for this purpose-- I mean, made available, because it was sitting empty-- by the owner of this enterprise, who was a Col.
Butlin, from Canada, who a few years earlier, had the idea that the British working classes are entitled to some cheap vacations by the seaside, too.
Why should the upper classes have it all?
And so he built these villages, which were really just basic shacks, with a bed.
There were three or four to a-- to a room, I think-- in some cases, two.
I forget the exact details.
And they were right by the seaside, scattered.
There were several of them.
I don't know how many.
We went to the one that was called Dovercourt, just by the-- along the cost from Harwich.
And of course, these were to be used in the summer, only.
Or they must have an exaggerated idea of the virtues of the English summer, as I later found out.
However, that would be at least bearable.
It doesn't get that cold.

Doesn't get so cold that the-- that the water in your hot-water bottle, which was the only means of heating, personal heating, available to us-- I'd never seen a hot-water bottle before, but I did in England-- that water froze, not solid.
But it started to freeze, and my feet with it.
So that was the hardest winter, it seems, in 80 years.
Didn't matter, didn't matter.
We were out of Germany.
Didn't matter at all, that was nothing.
And they had the usual.
You know, there was a mess hall and there was an entertainment hall.
And-- and we had briefings, and we had interviews with prospective foster parents, committee representatives.

The main Jewish committee had set up local committees, made up of-- well, mainly of Jewish businessmen from the area, who were able to finance the, the, the hostels that they were-- they would each set up a hostel in the town.
And they would house and feed the, the children, until such time-- send them to school-- until such time as-- we would see.
Because everybody knew that the war would start, and we would-- that things would then have to be changed in some way.
But we'll worry about that later.
For now, here's food, here's shelter.
So, so we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were there.
It was a kind of a transit camp, if you will.
And-- though as I say, it was pretty cold, it was-- I didn't hear anybody complain about it, [LAUGHS] not seriously.
There was a bit of bitching here and there, but that's all.
[DRINKS] How far away was that from London?
Just to get a sense of distance.
I would say two to three hours ride, by car.
By train, also.
Yes, quite a few.
No, I'm afraid I don't.
I have it written down.
I have some names written down, somewhere.
And I kept in touch with some, including one that went to a kibbutz-- ended up on a kibbutz in Israel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before it was Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, no.
It just half came to me, but-- but then we lost touch, after that.
But we stayed in touch for some years.
Lots, yes, lots.
Mainly shared-- sharing experiences-- how bad was it with you, how was it with you, et cetera, et cetera.
I was the envy of everybody in my compartment, because I'd been to a Jewish school the last couple of years.
Oh-- great.
And I knew what they had been going through.
They were all from-- no, they weren't all from Berlin.
They were-- Berlin was that collecting point.
Most of them were from Berlin, but there were quite a few from towns from which they had been sent to Berlin-- or taken-- taken by their parents to Berlin, and then put on the train.
There was another-- I learnt later, in fact, just recently-- there was another collecting point in Hamburg.
And there was also-- our train, by the way, was met-- was, was, was combined at [GERMAN], at the, at the border-- with a train from Vienna that brought Viennese children.
And they were less than when we-- maybe half or less, even-- a third, maybe.
So we arrived there as a mixed Berlin-Vienna bag-- we arrived in England.
I'd never met a Viennese before.
[LAUGHS]
No.
Oh, there were, there were.
You mean, like, counselors or something like-- there were, there were grownups around, yes, there were.
They were all English.
There was a rabbi, I'm sure, and, you know, one, one-- sure.
Yes, one could-- yes.
I should, I should amend that.
I should amend that.
Yes, yes.
The reason, probably, why I don't remember is that-- I don't remember that aspect of things-- is because I was 14.

And I think that the main demand for services of these comforting people would come from the younger ones-- would come from the ones who really didn't understand, didn't-- to the same extent that we older ones already did.

So that was one reason, in fact, why we didn't want to take up the time of the people-- of the rabbi and all these-- because they had their hands full with the ones that really needed it, which was the younger ones.
And were there any--
And we had some, some role in that, as well.
I mean, we-- somewhat, you know, took a little, somewhat, care of the younger ones, as far as we could.
But we weren't-- some were more used to it than others-- those with large families that had taken care of young, young siblings, were better at it than others.
Not that I can remember, no.
Until March of '39.
Three months, about.
About three months.
That makes it four months, doesn't it? 10th of-- we would arrive there 11th or 12th of December, '38, and-- I can find out the exact-- but it doesn't matter.
Three and a half months, or so, yeah.
Four months, maybe.
There were some rudimentary English lessons.
And in fact, the few of us that knew English, like I did, volunteered to give some English lessons to those that had none at all.
So we were given some books.
But it was very, very partial.
Because you know, we weren't teachers and hardly knew the language well enough to teach it to others.
But it was better than nothing, I imagine.
It made them feel a little bit that they could get around on their own, without-- without being total idiots.
And I think it did some good.
So there was some self-help going on, in this.
There was one very touching incident that I, I wanted to just quickly mention.
There was a girls boarding school nearby-- a very posh girls boarding school-- that for Christmas holidays, was of course empty, because all the, all the girls were, were-- had gone home.
And the directors of the school invited the children from the camp to, to go there and spend the Christmas holidays.
I think we were allowed to go for a week, because there weren't many more children in the camp than, than the school could take.
Although it was a fairly big school.
And I got-- that, that was a very nice experience.
Because it got us in touch-- we each had notes, when we went to the rooms-- our bedrooms-- that were the girls' bedrooms.
We each had a note from the girls, maybe with a little gift, and, you know, sort of expressing their pleasure in being able to do this and hoping they would-- that we would enjoy this break.
Well, we enjoyed the break-- in heated building, [LAUGHS] after, after-- out of the December and January winds howling outside.
So it was, it was a wonderful, a wonderful gift-- a good mitzvah-- that they did for us, there.
And we of course wrote notes back to the girls, secretly hoping that we could maybe make contact with them.
But I just wrote a note.

But with the lady who was the-- oh, I should also add that the, the teachers-- the senior teachers who were heads of houses, boarding schools are divided into houses, and each of them has a housemistress, and she is the boss of that house.
And the housemistresses stayed behind, and the staff stayed behind, to cook for us and to clean and so on.
And the housemistress stayed behind, to look after the whole caboodle.
And the one that-- I guess mostly because I spoke English-- that had our house-- I was made a prefect, because I spoke English.
So I was the intermediary between the boys and, and the, and the lady-- the, the housemistress.
And we got to know each other very well.
And she turned out to be just a wonderful person, who kept writing to me, sending me presents, throughout my years in, in England, actually.

And who, when I went to London, offered me her house that-- in Dulwich, suburb of London-- to stay in, because I didn't have a place to stay yet, at that time when I left the, the, the-- guardianship, or the-- not the 'guardianship,' the hostel.
They, they let me go on my own, and I went to London.
But I didn't have a job, which I got very quickly.
And I didn't have a place to stay, and so I stayed with her for a while-- her and her brother, who was a teacher at Dulwich College, which was a public school-- meaning private school-- in Dulwich.
So that was a very touching thing.
The whole thing was a very positive thing.
It was, it was a contact for me.
It was a human contact.
And, of course, I didn't know anybody otherwise in England.
Was there any religious practice at all, during that three-month period while you were there?
[DRINKS] Yes, there was some.
But I think it was Orthodox only.
I don't think then that-- maybe for that reason, there wasn't great participation.
It was seen as something apart.
I think what-- they had an orthodox rabbi only, and, and that, that was that.
And children that were not brought up in that didn't want to-- didn't know-- they just didn't know what to do.
They didn't know the, the routine.
Good question.
Sorry, I can't answer that.
I don't know.
There must have been, well, no.
There must have been.
I guess there are dispensations.
Oh, yes, there were services.
There were services.
No.
I went to Bradford, Yorkshire, from there, with another group that would-- we all would live in the same hostel, a group of boys between-- about the same age group, starting between 12 and 14, 15.
And we would all live in a large house that had been either rented or leased or bought by the Bradford committee, Jewish committee, who would be henceforth our guardians.
And that was presided over by a rabbi from either Germany or Austria-- I think Germany-- who was also a refugee.
And his family was there, of course.
And he was the 'warden,' as they called it, of the, of the, of the hostel.
Auerbach.
A-U-E-R-B-A-C-H.
No, no, he was a refugee rabbi.
He was a refugee who was rabbi.
He was not a a local rabbi, no, or an English rabbi.
I lived there for-- from March '39 until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] summer-- April, May-- of '42.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Three years, almost-- a little more than three years.
Yes.
As soon as I had an address, or even before, I wrote to them.
I wrote them from, from Holland.
I wrote them from, from the camp.
And, of course, they knew that I wouldn't have a permanent address yet.
They did send me some letters to the camp.
And you know, asking the kind of questions that they would.
How were things going, was I finding any, anybody-- how is this thing organized, because they had, they had very little idea how the thing was organized on the English side.
And-- which was doubly tough for them, because they had really no idea what they were sending me into, except it was a free country.
[LAUGHS] That was the main thing.
And the-- no, it was, there were family letters.
And as soon as I had, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as soon as I was in Bradford and had a more or less permanent address, I-- we, we had frequent correspondence.
Which I still have, I still have most of it.
At least weekly, I would say.
Until the outbreak of war, which wasn't very far away, which was only six months away.
March to September.
No, no.
I wouldn't-- I was-- Yes, yes.

Not a feeling, I would know that the Nazis were-- not necessarily censoring as in wartime, but they would certainly feel free-- somebody with a Jewish name, they would tear it open and, and censor it.
Yes.
Yes.
One had to tone down certain things, and they couldn't call things by the-- by the names they would call them to me in conversation, about things that were happening.
The laws, the, the the various horrors.
And-- but they were able to convey it quite well.
Yes.
I did.
I was able-- again, because I knew English-- to go to a secondary school.
And I stayed in that secondary school until it was evacuated, at the beginning of the war, in December, to be precise.
So it wasn't even a year that I was there.
There, the whole school was evacuated-- all schools were evacuated from cities and into the country.
There was a village school.
And classes were held in one large room, by sitting in corners, in groups, and trying not to, not to hear the echoes and the conversations from the other groups.
